cards.back: y(0⁺) = lim(s→∞) s·Y(s). Multiply by s, let s→∞.
y(∞) = lim(s→0) s·Y(s). Multiply by s, let s→0. Only valid if final value exists!
High s ↔ high frequency ↔ rapid changes ↔ initial time behaviour. Large s captures what happens right at t=0.
Small s ↔ low frequency ↔ DC ↔ steady state. s→0 captures the constant (final) part of the signal.

cards.front: IVT for RC step response Y(s)=1/[s(1+RCs)]?

cards.back: y(∞) = lim(s→0) 1/(1+RCs) = 1. Capacitor charges to full input voltage. ✓
When sY(s) has poles with Re(s)≥0: unstable systems, sustained oscillation. No final value → FVT invalid.

cards.front: Can FVT be used for sin(ωt)?
cards.back: No! Poles at ±jω on imaginary axis. Signal oscillates forever. No final value exists.
Both = 0. y(0)=0: inductor blocks sudden current. y(∞)=0: capacitor charged, no current. Current is transient only.

cards.front: RLC capacitor voltage: y(0⁺) and y(∞)?
cards.back: y(0)=0: capacitor uncharged. y(∞)=1: capacitor charges to input voltage at DC.

cards.front: How to verify your time-domain answer?
cards.back: Check: y(0) from your formula should match IVT result, y(∞) should match FVT result. If not → error in your PFE or inverse Laplace.
y(∞) = lim(s→0) 5/(s²+3s+2) = 5/2. Check: poles of sY(s) at s=−1,−2 (both LHP) → FVT valid.
